== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社}} in {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|ja|加東市}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional {{ill|Japanese performing art|ja|日本の伝統芸能}} dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能楽}}, while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽}}, {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃}}, and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|ja|法華経}}. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺}} and {{ill|Kofukuji|ja|興福寺}} indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as {{ill|Shunie|ja|修二会}}, a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社}} in {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|ja|加東市}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.
== Related Articles == * [[Okina]]

{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese traditional arts]] [[Category:Okina-mai]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Okina-mai]] [[sn:kina-mai]] [[en:Okina-mai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:おきなまい}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okina-mai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okina-mai{{DEFAULTSORT:{{substt:PAGENAME}}

[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional {{ill|Japanese performing art|ja|日本の伝統芸能|12=draft}} dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能楽|12=draft}}, while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|12=draft}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|12=draft}}, as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|12=draft}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|ja|法華経|12=draft}}. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=draft}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=draft}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kofukuji|ja|興福寺|12=draft}} indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as {{ill|Shunie|ja|修二会|12=draft}}, a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能|12=draft}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|ja|加東市|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=draft}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

It is regarded as the origin of modern {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|ja|法華経|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kofukuji|ja|興福寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as {{ill|Shunie|ja|修二会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh theater|ja|能|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.

[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|qq=}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|qq=}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|qq=}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|qq=}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|qq=}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

revision.comment: 13 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:翁舞 up to rev 101331157
revision.text:
[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[奈良豆比古神社の翁舞]]のうち太夫と脇の三人舞（2015年）]]
'''翁舞'''（おきなまい）は、日本の[[伝統芸能]]の[[舞]]。現在の[[能楽]]の原典とされる他、[[民俗芸能]]として各地に伝えられている。長寿の[[翁]]が人々の安寧を祈って舞う。
== 解説 == 古くは[[田楽]]や[[猿楽]]、あるいは[[人形浄瑠璃]]、[[歌舞伎]]、また民俗芸能などでも演じられる[[儀式]]的祝言曲であり、[[芸能]]本来の目的の一つに人の延命を願うことがあるが、その表現として翁媼を登場させることがあったものと考えられている。しかし、[[面]]を付け[[舞]]や[[語りもの|語り]]を演じる芸能は猿楽が最初であり、翁猿楽とか[[式三番]]と称された。翁猿楽の成立については、『法華五部九巻書』序品第一に、父叟は仏を、翁は文殊を、三番は弥勒をかたどるなどの[[仏教]]的解説があり、[[平安時代]]後期には成立していたとする説もあるが、『法華五部九巻書』を[[偽書]]とする説もある。確実な[[史料]]としては、[[弘安]]6年（[[1283年]]）の「春日若宮の臨時祭礼記」があげられ、舞楽・田楽・細男とともに、児・翁面・三番猿楽・冠者・父允を一組とする翁猿楽が演じられたとされており、このころ翁舞の一つの形式が成立した。
この翁の芸能が、どのような系譜に根ざしているか不明な部分も多いが、『[[大乗院寺社雑事記]]』や『興福寺明王院記録』によると、[[興福寺]][[修二会]]での[[鎮守神]]たる春日大宮の前で演じる猿楽が呪師走りと称され、また[[延暦寺]]の[[修正会]]でも、鎮守[[日吉大社]]で翁舞が演じられるなどの例があり、平安時代中期以降に大寺社の修正会・修二会などに[[守護神]]を祀る後戸で演じられた呪師猿楽の芸能として発展したものと推測されている。呪師の芸と翁舞の内容が「天下安全五穀豊穣」を祈願することでは共通していることから、修二会に奉仕する呪師の芸に始まりのちに猿楽者が呪師に代わって演じるようになったものと考えられている。[[室町時代]]の猿楽の[[座]]は、この[[宗教]]色の濃い翁猿楽を本芸として各地の[[寺社]]の[[祭礼]]に参勤し楽頭職を得ていたとされ、今日の能楽は、その[[余興]]芸とも言える猿楽能が人気を得て集大成されたものとされている<ref>『日本史大事典 1』、1154頁</ref>。
== 民俗芸能 == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|[[車大歳神社の翁舞]]（2006年）]] また、その古い形態を残す翁舞を民俗芸能として伝える地が数多くある。[[奈良県]][[奈良市]]奈良坂町にある[[奈良豆比古神社]]に伝わる[[奈良豆比古神社の翁舞|翁舞]]は、三人の翁が登場して舞うが、舞姿に残る古風は得がたい。[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]][[須磨区]]の[[車大歳神社]]の[[車大歳神社の翁舞|翁舞]]は室町時代に成立した[[能]]の[[翁 (能)|翁]]（式三番）が、千歳（露払い）・翁・[[三番叟]]（揉ノ段、鈴ノ段）という構成であるのに対し、露払い、翁、三番叟、父の尉で構成され、父の尉を省略しない古い形態を伝えている。兵庫県[[加東市]]上鴨川の住吉神社で行われる[[上鴨川住吉神社神事舞]]では、能の先行芸である呪師の芸の方固めにつながる、太刀舞・獅子・田楽舞・扇舞・高足など田楽ゆかりの芸も伝えられ、翁舞はいど・万蔵楽・六ぶん・翁・たからもの・冠者・父の尉の七つの舞からなる。初期の翁舞がそのままの形で承け継がれている稀有な例である<ref>『能・狂言図典』、79頁</ref>。
== 注 == <references />
== 参考資料 == * 青木和夫 網野善彦『日本史大事典 1』、[[平凡社]]、1992年、ISBN 4-582-13101-8 * 小林保治 森田拾史郎『能・狂言図典』[[小学館]]、1999年、ISBN 4-09-362062-8
== 関連項目 == * [[翁]]
{{日本の伝統芸能}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おきなまい}} [[Category:日本の伝統芸能]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[奈良豆比古神社の翁舞]]のうち太夫と脇の三人舞（2015年）]]
'''翁舞'''（おきなまい）は、日本の[[伝統芸能]]の[[舞]]。現在の[[能楽]]の原典とされる他、[[民俗芸能]]として各地に伝えられている。長寿の[[翁]]が人々の安寧を祈って舞う。
== 解説 == 古くは[[田楽]]や[[猿楽]]、あるいは[[人形浄瑠璃]]、[[歌舞伎]]、また民俗芸能などでも演じられる[[儀式]]的祝言曲であり、[[芸能]]本来の目的の一つに人の延命を願うことがあるが、その表現として翁媼を登場させることがあったものと考えられている。しかし、[[面]]を付け[[舞]]や[[語りもの|語り]]を演じる芸能は猿楽が最初であり、翁猿楽とか[[式三番]]と称された。翁猿楽の成立については、『法華五部九巻書』序品第一に、父叟は仏を、翁は文殊を、三番は弥勒をかたどるなどの[[仏教]]的解説があり、[[平安時代]]後期には成立していたとする説もあるが、『法華五部九巻書』を[[偽書]]とする説もある。確実な[[史料]]としては、[[弘安]]6年（[[1283年]]）の「春日若宮の臨時祭礼記」があげられ、舞楽・田楽・細男とともに、児・翁面・三番猿楽・冠者・父允を一組とする翁猿楽が演じられたとされており、このころ翁舞の一つの形式が成立した。
この翁の芸能が、どのような系譜に根ざしているか不明な部分も多いが、『[[大乗院寺社雑事記]]』や『興福寺明王院記録』によると、[[興福寺]][[修二会]]での[[鎮守神]]たる春日大宮の前で演じる猿楽が呪師走りと称され、また[[延暦寺]]の[[修正会]]でも、鎮守[[日吉大社]]で翁舞が演じられるなどの例があり、平安時代中期以降に大寺社の修正会・修二会などに[[守護神]]を祀る後戸で演じられた呪師猿楽の芸能として発展したものと推測されている。呪師の芸と翁舞の内容が「天下安全五穀豊穣」を祈願することでは共通していることから、修二会に奉仕する呪師の芸に始まりのちに猿楽者が呪師に代わって演じるようになったものと考えられている。[[室町時代]]の猿楽の[[座]]は、この[[宗教]]色の濃い翁猿楽を本芸として各地の[[寺社]]の[[祭礼]]に参勤し楽頭職を得ていたとされ、今日の能楽は、その[[余興]]芸とも言える猿楽能が人気を得て集大成されたものとされている<ref>『日本史大事典 1』、1154頁</ref>。

[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|qq=}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

revision.text:
[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|qq=}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.

[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|qq=}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11610451]] Wikidata name: okina mai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|qq=|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|qq=|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610451]] Wikidata name: okina mai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.

Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610451]] Wikidata name: okina mai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610451]] Wikidata name: okina mai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

<!--Okina-mai--><!--Okina-mai--><!--Okina-mai--><!--Okina-mai--> <!-- no translated page template found in history --> [[ja:翁舞]] {{translated page|ja|翁舞|version=101331157|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11610451}} [[Category:Added translation template]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Japanese traditional performing arts]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610451]] Wikidata name: okina mai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional [[:en:Theatre of Japan|Japanese performing art]] dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern [[:en:Nōgaku|Noh theater]], while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]], {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kofukuji]] indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as [[:en:Shuni-e|Shunie]], a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in [[:en:Noh|Noh theater]].
In [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō, Hyōgo]], the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō, Hyōgo}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.
