<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]]
[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional {{ill|Theatre of Japan|en|Theatre of Japan|WD=Q1364447|lt=Japanese performing art}} dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern {{ill|Nōgaku|en|Nōgaku|WD=Q1037119|lt=Noh theater}}, while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku}}, {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku}}, {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|en|Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=Lotus Sutra}}. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kofukuji}} indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as {{ill|Shuni-e|en|Shuni-e|WD=Q3482654|lt=Shunie}}, a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh theater}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō, Hyōgo}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh theater|1=Noh}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō, Hyōgo|1=Katō, Hyōgo}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:翁舞]]
[[File:20151002 Naratsuhiko jinja okinamai.jpg|thumb|340px|right|[[Naratsuhiko Shrine's Okina-mai]] featuring the dance of Tayu and two su11orting dancers (2015)]]
'''Okina-mai''' (翁舞) is a traditional {{ill|Theatre of Japan|en|Theatre of Japan|WD=Q1364447|lt=Japanese performing art|1=Theatre of Japan}} dance. It is regarded as the origin of modern {{ill|Nōgaku|en|Nōgaku|WD=Q1037119|lt=Noh theater|1=Nōgaku}}, while also being preserved as {{ill|folk performing art|ja|民俗芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk performing art|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 芸能|1=performing arts in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615913}} in various regions. The dance depicts an elderly figure, Okina, praying for the well-being and peace of people.
== Overview == Okina-mai has its roots in ritualistic celebratory performances from ancient {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}}, {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}}, {{ill|Bunraku|ja|人形浄瑠璃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunraku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文楽|1=Bunraku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q243170}}, and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, as well as in folk arts. Its purpose was often to express a wish for longevity, symbolized by the presence of an elderly man and woman. However, the earliest examples of performances involving masks, dance, and narration are attributed to Sarugaku, which was also known as "Okina Sarugaku" or {{ill|Shiki Sanban|ja|式三番|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiki Sanban|1=Shiki Sanban|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11254805}}.
The origins of Okina Sarugaku have been linked to Buddhist interpretations, such as in the preface of the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|en|Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}. In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kofukuji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as {{ill|Shuni-e|en|Shuni-e|WD=Q3482654|lt=Shunie|1=Shuni-e}}, a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh theater|1=Noh}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō, Hyōgo|1=Katō, Hyōgo}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
== Notes == <references />
== References == * Aoki Kazuo, Amino Yoshihiko, *Japan History Encyclopedia 1*, Heibonsha, 1992, ISBN 4-582-13101-8. * Kobayashi Yasuji, Morita Jushirō, *Noh and Kyogen Illustrated*, Shogakukan, 1999, ISBN 4-09-362062-8.

In this context, "Father" symbolizes the Buddha, "Okina" represents Manjushri, and the "Third Performer" corresponds to Maitreya. Some scholars suggest that Okina Sarugaku had already been established by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, but others question the authenticity of these interpretations, citing evidence from later documents such as the {{ill|Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja|法華五部九巻書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Gobu Kyūkan-sho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which some consider a forgery.
Reliable historical records, such as the {{ill|Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|ja|春日若宮おん祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Kasuga Wakamiya Festival|1=Kasuga Wakamiya On-Matsuri Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513690}} of 1283, describe Okina Sarugaku being performed alongside other art forms like court dances and dengaku. These performances included figures such as the child (Chigo), masked Okina, third performer, Crown Prince, and attendants, suggesting that the formal structure of Okina-mai was established by this period.
Although the lineage of Okina performing arts remains partially unclear, records from the {{ill|Daijoin Temple|ja|大乗院寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijoin Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kofukuji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} indicate that Sarugaku was performed in religious ceremonies such as {{ill|Shuni-e|en|Shuni-e|WD=Q3482654|lt=Shunie|1=Shuni-e}}, a Buddhist ritual. It is thought that Sarugaku actors initially replaced ritual specialists (Jushi) in these ceremonies, and their performances evolved into Okina-mai. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, Sarugaku troupes had adopted the religiously significant Okina-mai as a mainstay of their repertoire, performed at shrine and temple festivals across Japan.
== Folk Performing Arts == [[Image:Kuruma okina mai.jpg|thumbnail|340px|right|Okina-mai at [[Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine]] (2006)]] Several regions preserve ancient forms of Okina-mai as folk performing arts. At {{ill|Naratsuhiko Shrine|ja|奈良豆比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naratsuhiko Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}, the dance involves three Okina figures, preserving a distinct and ancient style. At {{ill|Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|ja|車大歳神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuruma Otoshigami Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, the dance includes roles such as Tsuruharai (usher), Okina, Senzan (third performer), and Chichi-no-Jō (Father of Elder). This structure predates the more condensed versions found in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh theater|1=Noh}}.
In {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō, Hyōgo|1=Katō, Hyōgo}}, the {{ill|Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|上鴨川住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Kamikawa Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉神社 (加東市上鴨川)}} preserves an elaborate Okina-mai ritual. Its structure includes seven dances: Ido, Manzōraku, Rokubun, Okina, Takaramono, Kanja, and Chichi-no-Jō. This rare example of a complete early Okina-mai ritual is considered invaluable for understanding its historical development.
